content.text: What Is a Golden Record Strategy for Agentic AI?

An agentic AI system acts on behalf of users across multiple steps, tools, and sessions. At every step it must reason about real-world entities: the customer it is serving, the product it is quoting, the policy it is applying. If those entities are represented inconsistently in the agent's context data—different names, conflicting attributes, stale states—the agent's reasoning will be flawed even if the underlying language model is sound.

A golden record strategy for agentic AI extends the classic MDM concept to the specific requirements of agent context: fast, structured access to canonical entity data that can be injected into prompts, used to resolve ambiguous user references, and relied upon as a consistent anchor across multi-step agent plans. The golden record is not just a data quality artifact—it is the agent's authoritative reference for every entity it touches.
Context Injection Architecture

The diagram below shows how golden records flow into agent context at request time: user identifies an entity, the context service resolves it to a golden record, and the serialized record is injected into the agent's prompt alongside the retrieved RAG chunks.

attrs.alt: Golden record injection flow: user request to entity resolver, golden record store, context serializer, prompt builder combining golden record with RAG chunks, agent LLM

content.text: The critical design decision is separating the golden record from RAG chunks in the prompt template. The golden record goes into a dedicated [ENTITY] section; RAG chunks go into [CONTEXT]. This gives the agent a clear hierarchy: when RAG context conflicts with the golden record, the golden record wins. When RAG context is absent, the golden record still provides authoritative facts.
Entity Reference Resolution

Users rarely refer to entities by their canonical MDM ID. They say "Product X", "our Berlin office", "the data retention policy". The agent must resolve these references to golden records before it can ground its reasoning. This resolution step is a first-class component of the agentic AI architecture, not an afterthought.

When resolution is ambiguous, the agent must ask a clarifying question rather than silently choosing a candidate. This is a hard requirement: an ambiguous resolution that proceeds without disambiguation will inject a wrong golden record into context, causing the agent to confidently provide information about the wrong entity.
Golden Record Serialization for Prompt Injection

Not all golden record attributes are relevant to every agent query. Injecting the entire golden record wastes context tokens and introduces irrelevant information that can confuse the model. The serializer selects the fields most relevant to the current query intent and formats them in a structured, readable way.

The query_intent parameter comes from an intent classifier that runs before the retrieval and context-building steps. Classifying intent before context injection is more efficient than injecting everything and relying on the model to ignore irrelevant fields — it reduces prompt length and avoids subtle attention artifacts from irrelevant structured data.
Multi-Entity Contexts in Agentic Plans

Agentic workflows often involve multiple entities in a single plan. A sales agent processing a quote needs the customer golden record, the product golden record, and the pricing policy golden record simultaneously. Managing multiple golden records in one context requires care to avoid context bloat and entity confusion.

Prioritize by relevance: At each step of the agent plan, serialize only the golden records directly relevant to that step's action. A step that queries inventory should not carry the customer golden record in context. The orchestrator should pass only the mdm_ids relevant to each step, not the full set for the plan.

Label entities clearly in the prompt: When multiple golden records are present, prefix each with a distinct label ([CUSTOMER], [PRODUCT], [POLICY]) so the model does not attribute a field from one entity to another. Label-based separation is more reliable than ordering alone when models have imperfect positional attention at large context lengths.

Entity reference anchoring: When the agent generates a plan that references entities across multiple steps, each plan step should specify the mdm_id it is acting on, not the user-provided alias. This prevents alias ambiguity from re-entering mid-plan. The orchestrator validates that referenced mdm_ids are in the golden record cache before executing each step.

Token budget for golden records: Set a per-entity token budget (e.g., 300 tokens per golden record) and a maximum number of golden records per prompt context (e.g., 3). When a plan requires more entities, split the plan into sub-tasks, each handling a subset of entities. Unlimited entity injection is a common cause of context overflow in production agentic systems.
Session-Level Golden Record Caching

In multi-turn agent sessions, the same entity is often referenced across many turns. Fetching the golden record from the MDM hub on every turn adds latency and increases load on the MDM system. A session-level cache holds the golden records resolved in the current session, with a TTL and a version-check on each access.

The version check before returning a cached record is the critical safety guard. Without it, a session that starts at version 5 of a golden record and runs for 10 minutes will continue using a stale version even if the MDM hub has advanced to version 6 due to a source system update mid-session. The TTL alone is not sufficient — version-based invalidation is required for high-risk domains.
Golden Record vs. RAG: Priority Rules

A common design question is: when the golden record and a RAG chunk disagree on a fact about the same entity, which should the agent trust? The answer depends on the domain and the type of disagreement.
Scenario
Recommended Priority
Rationale
Structured attribute (price, status, date)
Golden record wins
MDM survivorship rules have already resolved the authoritative value
Long-form text (product description, policy body)
RAG chunk wins
Full text is better retrieved through vector search than injected wholesale
Compliance rule or regulatory text
Golden record expiry/status wins; RAG provides body
Golden record knows if the policy is expired; RAG carries the content
Stale RAG chunk vs. current golden record
Golden record wins unconditionally
Version check at retrieval time flags the chunk; golden record provides current values
Alias Index Maintenance

The entity resolver depends on an alias index: a lookup table from common names, synonyms, abbreviations, and legacy identifiers to canonical mdm_ids. This index must be maintained as a first-class governance artifact alongside the golden records.
Sources of aliases: product model names, legacy part numbers, brand names, customer account nicknames, employee preferred names, abbreviations of department or office names, former policy titles.

Alias conflict detection: Two different golden records must not share the same exact alias in the same domain. When an alias conflict is detected, the resolver cannot produce a confident match and the agent will ask for clarification. Alias conflicts should be surfaced as data quality issues to domain stewards.

Alias retirement: When two golden records are merged, aliases from the retired record must be remapped to the surviving mdm_id, not deleted. Users may continue to refer to the merged entity by its old name for months after the merge.

Automated alias extraction: Aliases can be mined from conversation logs by collecting entity mentions that the agent successfully resolved, flagging unresolved mentions for steward review, and periodically re-training the alias index on resolved mentions. This creates a feedback loop between agent usage and alias quality.
Handling Golden Record Staleness Mid-Session

A golden record injected at the start of a session may become stale if the underlying entity is updated while the session is in progress. For most reference data this is acceptable, but for transactional data (prices, contract terms, inventory levels) mid-session staleness can cause real harm: the agent may commit to a price that is no longer valid.

Pre-commit version verification: Before the agent takes an action that relies on a golden record attribute (placing an order, quoting a price, confirming availability), the orchestrator re-fetches the current version of the golden record and compares it against the cached version. If the version has advanced, the agent re-evaluates its pending action with the updated data before committing.

Push invalidation via change events: The MDM hub can publish change events to a session pub-sub channel. The session orchestrator subscribes and invalidates its cache when a change event for a relevant mdm_id arrives. This is more responsive than polling and reduces the risk of acting on stale data between turns.

Reduced TTL for high-risk domains: Price, inventory, and contract term golden records should have a TTL of 60 seconds or less in the session cache. Descriptive fields like product descriptions can use TTLs of 30 minutes. The TTL policy should be set per entity_domain in the cache configuration, not as a global default.
Golden Record Governance and Stewardship

A golden record strategy only delivers reliable AI grounding if the records themselves are governed. Governance failures—undocumented survivorship rules, stale alias indexes, missing version history—directly translate to AI agent errors. The following stewardship practices are required for production deployments.

Domain steward assignment: Every entity domain must have a named steward accountable for survivorship rule accuracy, conflict resolution, and alias quality. The steward reviews the MDM hub's pending-conflict queue weekly. Without a named steward, conflicts accumulate and resolution quality degrades.

Golden record quality scorecard: Track the percentage of golden records with complete required attributes, the number of open conflicts awaiting steward resolution, the alias coverage rate (fraction of user mentions that resolve without ambiguity), and the staleness rate (fraction of chunks in the vector store whose golden_record_version lags the current version by more than the allowed threshold).

Agent-driven feedback loop: Log every entity resolution attempt: whether it succeeded, what confidence score it got, and whether the user corrected the agent's entity identification. Feed failed and low-confidence resolutions back to the alias index maintenance process. Corrections by users are high-value signals for both alias gaps and survivorship rule errors.

Change management for survivorship rules: Survivorship rules must be versioned and change-controlled. When a new source system is introduced or an existing system's reliability changes (e.g., the CRM is replaced), survivorship rules must be reviewed and updated before the new system's data flows into the grounding pipeline. A survivorship rule change is as consequential as a schema migration.
Testing the Golden Record Strategy
A golden record strategy should have automated tests at the integration layer to catch regressions before they affect production. Three test categories cover the main failure modes.

Resolution accuracy tests: A set of entity mention fixtures with known canonical mdm_ids. Run the entity resolver against each fixture and assert that the returned mdm_id and confidence score match expectations. Include ambiguous cases that must return ambiguous=True and cases with no match that must return mdm_id=None.

Serialization correctness tests: For each intent in INTENT_FIELD_MAP, verify that the serialized output contains exactly the expected fields and no others. Verify that the serialized text does not exceed the token budget. Verify that source attribution is present for every field. These tests catch accidental field leakage that could expose sensitive attributes.

Staleness detection tests: Simulate a golden record version advance mid-session and verify that the cache invalidates correctly. Simulate a change event arriving via pub-sub and verify that the cached record is invalidated. Verify that a pre-commit version check catches a mismatch and prevents the agent from committing the action. These are integration tests that require a mock MDM hub.
Key Takeaways

Golden records serve as the agent's authoritative reference for entity facts; they belong in a dedicated [ENTITY] section of the prompt, separate from RAG context, so the agent has a clear hierarchy to resolve conflicts.
Entity reference resolution from natural-language mentions to canonical mdm_ids must be a first-class component; ambiguous resolution must trigger clarification, not a silent best-guess.
Context serialization must be intent-aware: inject only the attributes relevant to the current query to avoid token waste and model confusion from irrelevant structured data.
Session-level caching reduces MDM hub load in multi-turn sessions but requires version-based invalidation, not TTL alone, for high-risk domains where the golden record may update mid-session.
The alias index is a governance artifact requiring the same stewardship rigor as the golden records themselves: conflict detection, retirement on merge, and feedback loops from agent resolution logs.
